coredata.dc:description: The influence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the physicochemical properties of polypyrrole nanoparticles (PpyNPs) was investigated experimentally and theoretically. The synthesis of the non-covalent PpyNPs/GQDs material was based on the known procedure of chemical pyrrole polymerization in the presence of a strong oxidant (ammonium persulfate, APS) and a surfactant (decyltrimethylammonium bromide, DeTAB). The obtained hybrids were in the form of spherical polymeric particles coated with GQDs with a diameter depending on the polymerization time and the amount of GQDs added during the synthesis. The morphology and porosity of the obtained materials were also influenced by the polymerization conditions, namely, composites with a higher GQD content showed higher BET specific surface area. On the other hand, the greatest attention was paid to the study of electrode processes taking place on the electrode modified with the PpyNPs/GQDs composite compared to the modification with pristine PpyNPs. GQDs incorporated into the polymeric network significantly change the energy level distribution at the interphase between Ppy and GQDs. Electronic interaction between both components results in GQDs participation in the Ppy-involved charge transfer processes. In consequence, oxidation of the mixed PpyNPs/GQDs system is much easier compared to the polymer itself. The charge transfer processes involving PpyNPs/GQDs composites are also much faster than those involving electroactive films formed only from PpyNPs. Additionally, a significant increase in the double-layer capacitance current is observed for composite materials. In conclusion, a clear improvement in the electrochemical behavior of polypyrrole in the composite material was observed, which was undoubtedly the result of the introduction of GQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR and (B) Raman spectra of PpyNPs (red line), GQDs (blue line) and PpyNPs/GQDs (black line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) DLS spectra recorded for PpyNPs and PpyNPs/GQDs (50) synthesized at different GQD addition times. (B) DLS spectra of PpyNPs/GQDs containing different amounts of GQDs added at selected time (4 + 8 h). Before DLS measurements, all samples were dispersed and sonicated in 1 M HCl.
TEM images of (A) GQDs, (B) PpyNPs and (C, D) PpyNPs/GQDs.
(A-C) SEM images recorded for PpyNPs/GQDs (50) synthesized at different GQD addition times. (D-F) SEM images of PpyNPs/GQDs containing different amounts of GQDs added at selected time (4 + 8 h).

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of (a) PpyNPs (red line), (b) PpyNPs/GQDs (150) (light gray line), (c) PpyNPs/GQDs (100) (dark gray line), and (d) PpyNPs/GQDs (50) (black line). Inset: BET surface area curves. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cyclic voltammograms of Au electrode modified with (a) PpyNPs (red line), (b) GQDs (blue line), (c) PpyNPs/GQDs (50) (yellow line), and (d) PpyNPs/GQDs (150) (green line), recorded in acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M TBAP at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Inset shows the log i- log E relationship for the first 10% of the oxidation peak high for electrode covered with PpyNPs (red line) and PpyNPs/GQDs (150) (green line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Dependence of the (A) capacity current at 0 V and (B) charge corresponding to the O2 peak on the amount of GQDs in the solution used for the composite formation.

Cyclic voltammograms of Au electrode modified with (A) PpyNPs/GQDs (50) and (B) PpyNPs/GQDs (150) recorded in acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M TBAP at different scan rates: (1) 10 mV s−1, (2) 25 mV s−1, (3) 50 mV s−1, (4) 100 mV s−1, and (5) 200 mV s−1. Insets: Linear dependence of anodic peak current on the sweep rate for PpyNPs/GQDs (50) and PpyNPs/GQDs (150).

Top views of the relaxed structures of Ppy/GQD. Carbon atoms of Ppy are brown, nitrogen atoms are pale blue, oxygen atoms are red and hydrogen atoms are pink. All carbon atoms from GQD are green for transparency. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Total and projected density of states (DOS) obtained from the DFT(PBE) + D3(BJ) calculations for Ppy/GQDs. The Fermi level is marked by the dashed line.
Synthetic procedure for obtaining polypyrrole nanoparticles modified with graphene quantum dots.
Textural parameters calculated from nitrogen adsorption/desorption studies.

head.ce:title: Enhancement of polypyrrole electrochemical performance with graphene quantum dots in polypyrrole nanoparticle/graphene quantum dot composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Bialystok, Ciolkowskiego 1K, 15-245 Bialystok, Poland
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, University of Bialystok, Ciolkowskiego 1K, 15-245 Bialystok, Poland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The influence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the physicochemical properties of polypyrrole nanoparticles (PpyNPs) was investigated experimentally and theoretically. The synthesis of the non-covalent PpyNPs/GQDs material was based on the known procedure of chemical pyrrole polymerization in the presence of a strong oxidant (ammonium persulfate, APS) and a surfactant (decyltrimethylammonium bromide, DeTAB). The obtained hybrids were in the form of spherical polymeric particles coated with GQDs with a diameter depending on the polymerization time and the amount of GQDs added during the synthesis. The morphology and porosity of the obtained materials were also influenced by the polymerization conditions, namely, composites with a higher GQD content showed higher BET specific surface area. On the other hand, the greatest attention was paid to the study of electrode processes taking place on the electrode modified with the PpyNPs/GQDs composite compared to the modification with pristine PpyNPs. GQDs incorporated into the polymeric network significantly change the energy level distribution at the interphase between Ppy and GQDs. Electronic interaction between both components results in GQDs participation in the Ppy-involved charge transfer processes. In consequence, oxidation of the mixed PpyNPs/GQDs system is much easier compared to the polymer itself. The charge transfer processes involving PpyNPs/GQDs composites are also much faster than those involving electroactive films formed only from PpyNPs. Additionally, a significant increase in the double-layer capacitance current is observed for composite materials. In conclusion, a clear improvement in the electrochemical behavior of polypyrrole in the composite material was observed, which was undoubtedly the result of the introduction of GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to excellent optical and electronic properties, conductive polymers are widely used in various fields of science and technology. They can be applied in electronic devices [1–3], solar cells [4–6], light emitting devices [7,8], sensors and biosensors [9–11] and so on. Many of these applications depend on the electrochemical properties of conducting polymers or materials containing conducting polymers as one of the components. Typical conducting polymers, such as polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole (Ppy), polythiophene (PTH) and their derivatives, exhibit electronic conductivity due to the double bond conjugation. The conductivity of these polymers significantly increases upon oxidation or reduction. The extra charge (hole or electron) is delocalized over a few monomer units to form polarons or negative polarons, depending on the nature of the redox process. This extra charge is transported through the polymeric chain. Additionally, polymer oxidation or reduction processes are accompanied by the transport of supporting electrolyte ions to maintain the neutrality of the solid polymeric phase. All the mentioned processes related to electrode reactions involving polymers strongly depend on the structure, composition and morphology of the solid phase containing the conductive polymers.

Polypyrrole is one of the conducting polymers very often used in electrical energy storage systems because of their fast electrochemical switching, good redox reversibility, high pseudocapacitance values and environment-friendly properties [12,13]. It can be easily chemically or electrochemically formed from commerciallly available monomers and simple synthetic procedures [14]. Deposition of Ppy at different substrates including metallic surfaces [15], semiconducting material surfaces [16] and carbon-based rigid and flexible materials have been reported [17,18]. Ppy can be obtained in the form of various structures which vary in shape, size and physicochemical properties. The nanoparticle materials are particularly useful in the formation of electrochemical capacitors [19] and batteries [20]. The micro- and nanostructuring of the polymeric phase leads to a significant increase in the active surface of the electroactive material. An increase in the amount of polymeric material involved in electrode processes and easier transport of counterions of the supporting electrolyte can be expected for materials formed from such nano- or microstructures [21–25]. The one-dimensional polymeric structures are particularly useful in electrotechnology [24,26–30]. They exhibit high aspect ratio, highly porous network, open channels for enhanced mass transport, better interconnections, and larger surface area interactions. Such structures are commonly used as electroactive materials in supercapacitors [31–33] or batteries [34–37]. In contrast, spherical polymeric particles are less useful due to their tendency to agglomerate. Such a process negatively influences the electrochemical performance of polymeric materials. To overcome such agglomeration processes, spherical polymeric particles can be incorporated into the network of carbon nanomaterials, such as single- and multi-walled carbon nanotubes [38–41] or graphene-based structures [39,42–47]. These carbon nanomaterials provide porous and conducting network for polymeric nanoparticles enabling orderly and fast flow of electrons, easy transfer of ions and ensuring high efficiency of electrode processes involving polymer material.

In the case of composites composed of conducting polymers and carbon nanotubes, the morphology of the composite material depends on the synthesis procedure. A simple composite formation by the drop coating method from the solution containing dispersed polymeric spherical nanoparticles and carbon nanotubes results in the formation of irregular structures with polymeric particles randomly distributed within the carbon nanotube network [48,49]. A core-shell structure of carbon nanotubes coated with conducting polymer can be obtained by polymerization of pyrrole in the solution containing dispersed nanotubes. The synthesis carried out in the presence of surface-active compounds leads to the formation of nanotube structures that are relatively homogeneously covered with a polymer layer [50–52]. Recently, it has been reported that oxidized carbon nanotubes can be used as an oxidation agent in formation of multi-walled carbon nanotubes and polypyrrole composites with uniform thickness of polymeric layer [53].

Graphene and graphene derivatives have also become an attractive platform for the immobilization of polymeric nanoparticles. Graphene is a monolayer of graphite consisting of sp2-bonded carbons with a honeycomb structure. Its metallic character ensures the high electronic conductivity of the formed composite. However, due to the strong π–π stacking interaction, graphene materials tend to form agglomerated structures of nanoplatelets with a typical thickness of 2–10 graphene layers [54]. Hybridization of graphene and conducting polymers results in the formation of a variety of composites that exhibit enhanced electrochemical performance [38,55–59]. As with oxidized carbon nanotubes, oxidized graphene can initiate the formation of conductive polymers on its surface [60].

Recently, much attention has been given to composites of conductive polymers with quantum nanostructures. Various structures of several nm in size have been incorporated into conductive polymeric materials. Molybdenum nitride quantum dots have been embedded in polyaniline to form novel electrode material for supercapacitor [61]. Semiconducting quantum dots, such as CdS or CsPbI3 incorporated into Ppy [62,63] or PANI [64] significantly modify photochemical properties of polymeric materials and enhance photoinduced charge transport through the composite layer. Polymeric material can also enhance stability of semiconducting quantum dots. Such behavior was reported for composite of Ppy and CdSe/ZnS quantum nanoparticles [65]. Composite of molybdenum sulfide and PANI can be used for biosensor construction [66] or for in vivo dual-modal imaging guided synergistic photothermal/radiation therapy of cancer [67]. Metallic quantum dots can also significantly influence charge transfer processes in polymeric materials [68–70]. Such systems are often used in the construction of biosensors. For example, composites of PANI and Ppy with gold particles a few nm in size ware applied for glucose sensing [69,70]. However, the most attention was paid to composites of conductive polymers and carbon-based quantum dots (carbon quantum dots, CQDs and graphene quantum dots, GQDs). Both of these structures are composed of graphene sheets a few nm in size. GQDs are formed primarily by sp2 hybridized atoms, while CQDs exhibit an amorphous structure dominated by sp3 hybridization [71,72]. Therefore, GQDs show a much more regular 2-D structure and exhibit higher reactivity than CQDs. The major advantage of carbon-based quantum dots, particularly GQDs, over graphene, carbon nanotubes and other larger carbon nanostructures is their good dispersibility both in water and in various non-aqueous solvents.

The structures of GQD/Ppy [73–78] and GQD/PANI [27,79–82] composites and their electrochemical properties have been investigated. In most cases, composites were prepared through in situ polymerization in a GQD suspension. Particular attention was given to the capacitive properties of composites with GQDs and Ppy or PANI, observing a significant increase in the specific capacity [27,73–76,79–82] and cycle stability [27,73,80] of composite electroactive materials in comparison to pure conducting polymers. Such behavior is related to the enhancement of the double-layer component of capacitance, structural modification of the electroactive material or participation of GQDs in the charge transfer processes within the electrode material. Chemical modification of the GQDs structure may also lead to an improvement in the capacitance properties of composites of these nanostructures with conductive polymers. Covalent modification of GQDs with ferrocene (Fc) moieties results in enhancement of capacitance properties of Fc-GQD/Ppy composite [75]. Sulfonated GQDs can be efficiently incorporated onto Ppy structure by electrostatic interaction to form creating a high-capacity electroactive composite [73]. Doping GQDs with heteroatoms, such as B, N and S is another method leading to change in the charge distribution in carbon nanostructures and, as a result, energy of their interaction with conducting polymers. Very high capacities of 2500 F g−1, significantly exceeding the value of the theoretical specific capacitance of PANI, were reported for the composite of PANI and GQDs co-doped with N and S [81].

In this work, we investigated the effect of GQDs on the morphological and electrochemical properties of spherical polypyrrole nanoparticles (PpyNPs). In the forming composite, the surface of polymer nanoparticles is decorated with GQDs. Decoration of the PpyNPs surface with GQDs results in nanoparticle size reduction during the polymerization process. The electrochemical studies presented so far in the literature have been devoted to the macroscopic properties of composites with the participation of GQDs. In this work, the results of electrochemical measurements were interpreted based on theoretical calculations of the distribution of energy levels at the interface between the two components of the composite. The electronic interaction between Ppy network and GQDs significantly influences the electrochemical response of the polymeric component. The presence of GQDs in the composite material increases the rate of charge transfer processes through the electroactive layer. Additionally, a strong electronic interaction between both composite components significantly influences the electron transfer processes at the interphase between polymer and GQDs. In consequence, oxidation of the Ppy layer GQDs system is much easier compared to the polymer itself. In consequence, the oxidation of the polypyrrole layer in direct contact with GQDs requires much less energy compared to bulk Ppy phase.

All chemicals used in this work were commercially available: citric acid (pure p.a., Pol-Aura); pyrrole (98%, Aldrich); decyltrimethylammonium bromide (≥98%, Sigma-Aldrich); ammonium persulfate (98%, Sigma-Aldrich); potassium bromide (≥99%, Sigma-Aldrich); tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (≥95%, Sigma-Aldrich); hydrochloric acid (35–38%, Pol-Aura); ethanol (≥96%, POCH); acetonitrile (99.8%, Sigma-Aldrich); dichloromethane (≥99.8%, Sigma-Aldrich).

GQDs synthesis was performed according to the procedure described elsewhere [83]. Two grams of citric acid was subjected to pyrolysis at the temperature of 200 °C under intensive stirring conditions. Approximately 5 min after reaching the desired temperature, the powder was observed to turn into a colourless liquid. Shortly thereafter, the resulting liquid turned yellow and then, after approximately 30 min, yellowish orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. The obtained product was diluted with 250 mL of distilled water, and the solvent was evaporated at 100 °C.

PpyNPs were obtained in the process of chemical polymerization of pyrrole in the presence of a strong oxidant and surfactant [84]. Some of the synthesis conditions, i.e., the concentration of surfactant and the polymerization time were optimized to obtain well-separated spherical nanoparticles. In the chosen procedure, 0.04 g of pyrrole was added slowly to 35 mL of the decyltrimethylammonium bromide aqueous solution (DeTAB; 0.094 M). The resulting mixture was then cooled in an ice bath on a magnetic stirrer for 10 min. Next, 5 mL of a pre-cooled ammonium persulfate solution (APS; 0.12 M) was added dropwise. The reaction mixture was stirred at 0 °C for 12 h. The obtained product was washed three times with ethanol and water and finally dried in air at room temperature.

Preparation of the PpyNPs/GQDs composite was carried out analogously to the PpyNPs synthesis procedure described in the previous subsection. 0.04 g of pyrrole was added slowly to 35 mL of DeTAB solution (0.094 M). The mixture was cooled in an ice bath on a stirrer for 10 min, and then 5 mL of precooled APS solution (0.12 M) was added dropwise. GQDs were introduced into the reaction mixture in various amounts and at different times to study the actual influence of the added component on the physicochemical properties of the synthesized composite. For this purpose, the tested amount of GQDs (50, 100 or 150 mg) was dissolved in 1 mL of distilled water, and then the obtained solution was introduced into the mixture to form polypyrrole nanoparticles 4, 6 or 11 h after the addition of APS oxidant. Next, the reaction mixture was stirred at 0 °C for 8, 6 or 1 h (the entire process lasted 12 h in total). The obtained composites were washed three times with ethanol and water and finally dried in air at room temperature.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) studies were performed using a Nicolet IN10 MX microscope (Thermo Scientific, USA). The FTIR spectra were recorded with a KBr pellet in transmission mode and collected with a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range 400–4000 cm−1. The room-temperature Raman spectra were recorded with an inVia confocal microscope (Renishaw, United Kingdom) with a spectral resolution of 2 cm−1 using an Ar laser at λ = 785 nm. Particle size measurements were performed using the dynamic light scattering method (DLS) on a NanoPlus™ 3 Zeta Nanoparticle Analyser (Particulate Systems, a brand of Micromeritics Instrument Corp., USA).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were performed using an INSPECT S50 microscope (FEI, Japan). The accelerating voltage of the electron beam was 10 keV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a Tecnai G2 20 X-TWIN (FEI, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

Nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements were performed at − 196 °C using an ASAP 2020 analyser (Micromeritics Corp., USA). Prior to measurements, the samples were degassed for 12 h at 120 °C under reduced pressure.

Cyclic voltammetry was performed using a PGSTAT 302 N potentiostat (AUTOLAB, The Netherlands) with a three-electrode system, Au (Ø 3 mm) as the working electrode, Pt mesh (0.25 mm) as the counter electrode, and Ag/Ag+ as the reference electrode. Before the measurements, the surface of the Au electrode was polished and modified by applying 20 μL of the synthesized material solution (0.5 mg mL−1 in dichloromethane for PpyNPs and composites; 1.5 mg mL−1 in dichloromethane for pure GQDs) and then the solvent evaporated. All measurements were carried out in acetonitrile solution containing 0.1 M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP).

The first-principle DFT calculations were performed using the Quantum ESPRESSO package [85] in conjunction with the ultrasoft pseudopotentials [86]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [87] with added van der Waals corrections [88] was used to describe the exchange–correlation effect. This approach corrects the total energy by a pairwise term, which accounts for dispersion interactions. A plane-wave cut-off energy of 30 Ry was used. The relaxation procedure was stopped when the Hellmann-Feynman forces on all atoms were smaller than 0.01 eV/Å. The convergence criterion for the electronic self-consistent iteration was set to be lower than 1.4 meV. For the mixed Ppy/GQDs system, the Brillouin zone was sampled at the Γ point. For density of states (DOS) calculations, the Monkhorst-Pack [89] scheme with a 4 × 4 × 1 k-point convergence grid was used. In addition, the peak width for broadening was set to 0.1 eV for better visualization of the DOS. Spin-polarization was taken into account for GQD and Ppy/GQDs structures.

The procedure for the formation of the PpyNPs/GQDs composite is presented in Scheme 1 (detailed experimental conditions are described in section 2.2.3.). In the first step, it was crucial to mix the monomer (pyrrole) and surfactant (DeTAB) as evenly as possible and to lower the temperature to 0 °C. The addition of an oxidant (APS) in the next step caused the polymerization process to start. Up to this point, the procedure was the same as for the synthesis of pristine PpyNPs. The last and most important step was to introduce GQDs into the reaction mixture and to optimize the polymerization time and the amount of added carbon nanostructures.

As a result, the synthesized hybrids were in the form of polymeric spheres modified with GQDs, which were partially enclosed inside the structure of Ppy nanoparticles, and partially decorated their surfaces. The obtained non-covalent system was based on the occurrence of interactions between GQDs rings and heterocyclic systems present in the structure of the polypyrrole. Moreover, the course of the nanoparticle polymerization process in the presence of selected carbon nanostructures was characterized by greater control due to the size and chemical nature of GQDs. The presence of functional groups at the edges of GQDs increased their hydrophilicity, and the mutual electrostatic repulsion of their structures allowed them to act as an additional surfactant. This increased the colloidal stability of the reaction mixture.

FTIR and Raman spectroscopy were used to characterize the structure and composition of the nanoparticles synthesized by the oxidation of pyrrole in the presence of a strong oxidant (APS), surfactant (DeTAB) and graphene quantum dots. Fig. 1 A shows the infrared spectra recorded for pristine PpyNPs and GQDs as references, as well as for the PpyNPs/GQDs composite material.

The position of the signals in the spectrum of pristine nanoparticles (Fig. 1A, red line) corresponds to the bands characteristic of the polypyrrole structure [90-93] [69–72, 90–93], which indicates the successful formation of this polymer. The strong band observed at approximately 1550 cm−1 confirms the presence of CC stretching and CC in-ring vibrations. Other important peaks at 1471, 1192, and 1043 cm−1 correspond to CN stretching vibrations, CH in-plane deformation vibrations, also known as breathing vibrations, and CC out-of-plane deformation vibrations, respectively. In turn, a strong sharp band occurring at 924 cm−1 is assigned to CH out-of-plane deformation vibrations. Moreover, the weak signal observed at approximately 1710 cm−1 confirms the presence of carbonyl groups attached to the pyrrole rings, most likely due to the use of a strong oxidant (APS) [91]. On the other hand, the bands at 2904 and 2841 cm−1, corresponding to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of –CH2, respectively, may indicate trace amounts of the DeTAB not washed out from the surface of the obtained PpyNPs [94].

The FTIR spectrum obtained for GQDs (Fig. 1A, blue line) consists of bands confirming their graphene-like structure (e.g., CC backbone vibrations at 1625 cm−1) and the presence of oxygen-containing groups at their edges (e.g., OH stretching vibrations at 3438 cm−1, CO stretching vibrations at 1716 cm−1, C-OH stretching vibrations at 1176 cm−1) [95–97]. Signals characteristic of both components are clearly observed in the spectrum recorded for PpyNPs/GQDs (Fig. 1A, black line); however, they differ slightly in intensity and position compared to the spectra of references. This is due to the mass participation of individual components in the composite, the overlapping of bands occurring at a similar wavenumber, and the π-π interactions between the polypyrrole and GQDs [98].

The Raman spectra presented in Fig. 1B completed the spectroscopic studies on the structure of the obtained materials. Two strong signals are observed in the spectrum recorded for GQDs (Fig. 1B, blue line), namely, the G band at 1601 cm−1, indicating an ordered planar structure of the sp2 carbon network, and the D band at 1360 cm−1, corresponding to sp3 structural defects (e.g., edges, functional groups) [99]. The ID/IG band ratio calculated for the obtained GQDs can therefore inform about the participation of functional groups at the edges of the GQDs in relation to the CC bonds present in their structure [97]. The high intensity of both the G and D bands indicates a high order inside the GQD plane, but also confirms the presence of numerous oxygen-containing groups located mainly at the edges.

The position of some signals in the spectrum of PpyNPs (Fig. 1B, red line) is very similar to the position of the G and D bands, which can be explained by the presence of unsaturated (CC) and saturated (CC) groups in the rings of the tested polymer [91]. The characteristic bands observed at 949/991, 1053, 1336, 1420, and 1589 cm−1 were assigned to CC ring deformation, CH in-plane deformation, CC in-ring stretching, CC stretching, and CC in-ring stretching, respectively. This confirms the obtaining of polypyrrole in the form of nanoparticles [100].

The slight shifts in the position of the mentioned bands in the spectrum of PpyNPs/GQDs (Fig. 1B, black line) are most likely caused by π − π interactions between the polymer rings and carbon nanostructures. Moreover, a significant increase in the intensity of the bands at 1318 and 1374 cm−1 relative to the band intensity at 1600 cm−1 in comparison to the corresponding signals in the spectrum of unmodified nanoparticles (1336 and 1420 cm−1 vs band at 1589 cm−1) should be emphasized. A high content of sp3 hybridized carbons indicates an increase in the number of structural defects as a result of GQD addition [91].

Fig. 2 shows the DLS spectra of PpyNPs/GQDs composites synthesized under various experimental conditions and suspended in 1 M HCl. The results confirm that DeTAB used as a surfactant agent perfectly stabilizes and separates the obtained nanoparticles. The addition of GQDs to the mixture, in which the polymerization of pyrrole began, at different times, caused clear differences in the hydrodynamic diameter of the synthesized nanoparticles and their polydispersity (Panel A of Fig. 2). The later the GQDs were introduced into the reaction mixture, the larger the polymerized particles and the wider the distribution of their diameters. Comparing the DLS spectra obtained for the composites (Fig. 2A, black lines) with those obtained for unmodified PpyNPs (Fig. 2A, red line), it is clear that the presence of GQDs affects the polymerization process itself, contributing to the synthesis of smaller and more homogeneous nanoparticles, provided that GQDs are introduced in the initial stage of polymerization. Unmodified PpyNPs polymerized for 12 h achieved a diameter of approximately 546 nm, PpyNPs/GQDs (4 + 8 h) obtained by adding GQDs 4 h after the introduction of APS and maintained for another 8 h were approximately 190 nm in diameter, while PpyNPs/GQDs (6 + 6 h) and PpyNPs/GQDs (11 + 1 h) reached a diameter of 530 and 688 nm, respectively, which were values close to or greater than for pure PpyNPs.

The effect of GQDs added in the early stage of polymerization on the size of the synthesized nanoparticles most likely results from their ability to act as an additional surfactant. A similar assumption was made by Che Man et al., who used nanosized graphene oxide (GO) as a surfactant in the process of miniemulsion polymerization of styrene [101]. Both types of the aforementioned carbon nanostructures have hydrophilic functional groups at the edges, mainly carboxylic groups, which, combined with the hydrophobic nature of their planes, allows them to also be used as amphiphilic surfactants. The effect of GQDs and nanosized GO on increasing the colloidal stability of the reaction mixture is most likely due to the occurrence of electrostatic repulsion between carboxylic groups in contact with the edges of adjacent carbon nanostructures [101–103].

Moreover, the influence of the amount of GQDs added to the reaction mixture on the size achieved by the PpyNPs/GQDs using the optimal time (4 + 8 h) was also investigated (Fig. 2B). A significant increase in the diameter of composite nanoparticles was observed with an increase in the amount of GQDs introduced (from 50 to 150 mg). Some GQDs were presumably enclosed in the structure of nanoparticles, while some decorated their surface. Their share on the surface was proportional to the amount of nanostructures used; therefore, the more GQDs were introduced into the reaction, the greater the increase in the diameter of nanoparticles.

The sizes of the synthesized nanoparticles determined by the DLS method were hydrodynamic diameters and therefore overestimated in relation to the actual diameters. The obtained results were compared with the diameters determined from TEM images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microscopic investigations of the structure and morphology of synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images showing GQDs (Fig. 3 A) confirm the synthesis of well-separated nanosized (2–6 nm) spherical objects. The obtained PpyNPs also have a spherical shape, but their diameter is much larger (approximately 200 nm) (Fig. 3B). It should be emphasized, however, that the tendency of polypyrrole nanoparticles to agglomerate is important in this case; therefore, the sizes observed in the TEM images are most likely results from an aggregate composed of PpyNPs and not individual nanoparticles. Much better separation and much smaller agglomerates (approximately 100–150 nm) were obtained for the PpyNPs/GQDs composites (Fig. 3C). This suggested that the introduced GQDs, apart from the role of a composite component, also have an additional function as a surfactant, because of which the diameters of the composite material are smaller than for the PpyNPs. Due to the similar diffraction contrast characterizing the imaging of both PpyNPs and GQDs, on the TEM images taken for the composite at higher magnification (Fig. 3D), it was not possible to observe single dots. However, it was confirmed that the observed PpyNP/GQD spheres are agglomerates of many nanoparticles.

The morphology of the composite nanoparticles obtained at different times after the introduction of GQDs into the reaction mixture, as well as at different amounts of GQDs added, was examined by SEM microscopy (Fig. 4 ). For all the tested samples, a strong degree of aggregation of the material was observed. However, they differed in homogeneity and grain size. The SEM images confirmed obtaining the finest and most homogeneous structures in the case of adding GQDs 4 h after the start of polymerization (Fig. 4A). Extending the moment of introducing carbon nanostructures to 6 and 11 h resulted in the appearance of undesirable morphological forms, such as ribbons (Fig. 4B) or flakes (Fig. 4C). Moreover, the amount of GQDs introduced into the composite material did not have a significant influence on the homogeneity of the sample. On the other hand, the formation of finer-grained structures was observed when a larger amount of GQDs was used (Fig. 4F) than when smaller amounts were added (Fig. 4D–E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen adsorption/desorption characterization of synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of GQDs and their amounts on the porosity of PpyNP/GQD nanoparticles was investigated using the nitrogen adsorption/desorption method (Fig. 5 ). The specific surface area (SBET) was calculated through Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis, and the pore size distribution was determined by the Barret, Joyner, and Halenda (BJH) method. The course of the isotherms and BET curves presented in Fig. 5 clearly shows that pure PpyNPs have the largest specific surface area (SBET = 21 m2/g).

The reduction of the BET surface area for composite nanoparticles was probably due to the partial blocking of the pores by the introduced GQDs. It is interesting, however, that along with the increase in the content of GQDs, the specific surface area also increased. For PpyNPs/GQDs (50), PpyNPs/GQDs (100) and PpyNPs/GQDs (150), the SBET values ​​were 5, 7 and 9 m2/g, respectively (Table 1 ).

This can be explained by the fact that part of the GQDs modifying the surface of PpyNPs, and not blocking the pores slightly increased the specific surface area due to the micropores present in the GQD structure. This hypothesis was confirmed by the adsorption cumulative volume of pores values, which increased with increasing GQD content (Table 1). Moreover, due to the nature of the additional surfactant, the more GQDs were introduced into the reaction mixture, the smaller the size of the aggregates formed (from 1133 nm for PpyNPs/GQDs (50) to 649 nm for PpyNPs/GQDs (150); Table 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and computational investigations of synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of GQDs in the composite materials can significantly influence the electrochemical properties of the electrochemically active component of the composite. For example, such behaviour was observed for polyaniline nanotubes modified with GQDs [27]. The presence of the GQD component influences both the morphology and electronic properties of the composite material. Similar behaviour was observed for the composite composed of PpyNPs and GQDs. In Fig. 6 , the voltammetric behaviour of PpyNPs/GQDs composites containing different amounts of GQDs within the electroactive material is presented. For comparison, the electrochemical properties of electrodes modified with pristine PpyNPs and GQDs are presented. All results were obtained for the same amount of PpyNPs within the composite material deposited at the electrode surface. GQDs influence both the faradaic process of Ppy oxidation and the capacitance current recorded in the potential range less positively than the potentials of Ppy oxidation. For the faradaic process of PpyNP oxidation (O1), the dependence of the logarithm of the oxidation current on the potential for the first 10% of the high oxidation peak was analysed. The slope of such a log i – E relation is related to the rate of the polymer oxidation process. This slope is much higher in the case of the PpyNP/GQD composite in comparison to the value of log i – E slope obtained for the electrode modified with pristine PpyNPs. Despite the increase in the GQD amount in the composite, the peak O1 current density is almost the same.

A significant influence of GQDs is also observed in the potential range less positive in comparison to the potentials of the PpyNPs faradaic oxidation process. First, a large increase in capacitance current is observed, indicating a significant increase in the real surface area of the composite material with increasing GQD amount in the composite. A relationship between the capacitance current at 0 V and the amount of GQDs in the composite material is shown in panel A of Fig. 7 .

A new oxidation peak, O2, in this potential range is also recorded under voltammetric conditions. The current density of this peak increases with an increase in the amount of GQDs within the composite. Similar behaviour was observed for oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (ox-MWCNTs) and Ppy [53]. In the case of ox-MWCNT@Ppy composite, a surface-like peak at a potential of ca. −0.1 V vs Ag/AgCl is related to the oxidation process of the polypyrrole layer, which is in direct contact with the ox-MWCNT surface [53]. The nature of the O2 oxidation peak can be explained in a similar way. This result is related to the oxidation of polypyrrole from the PpyNPs/GQDs interphase. The charge corresponding to the O2 peak increases with an increase in the amount of GQDs in the composite material (Fig. 7B).

The peak current corresponding to the PpyNP oxidation process O1 linearly depends on the sweep rate. Such linear relationships obtained for composites containing two different amounts of GQDs are presented in Fig. 8 . Linear relationships between the anodic peak current (Ipa) and sweep rate (v) indicate that the surface material involved fast charge transfer processes. It was also observed that the electrochemical behaviour of the synthesized composites is stable upon potential cycling.

The results of electrochemical experiments correlate well with the theoretical calculations of the energy level distribution near the Fermi level in the mixed Ppy and GQD system. Periodic boundary conditions and a supercell approximation were applied to model GQD interactions with the surface of Ppy. The modelling procedure was divided into three steps. First, the unit cell shape of the Ppy bulk using variable-cell calculations was optimized, obtaining the orthorhombic lattice with a = 7.19 Å, b = 6.77 Å and c = 3.69 Å. Then, this cell was multiplied in the a-, b-direction six times and in the c-direction seven times and left only one layer in the supercell with 576 atoms. In our previous work [27], we modeled the structure of GQD based on the deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s XPS signal to properly describe the relative concentration of different oxygen-containing groups located mainly at the edges and in their close vicinity of the graphene plate. In this work, we also used this structure. Our GQD model (250 atoms) contains 11 carboxyl groups, 14 hydroxyl groups and 20 carbonyl groups in very good agreement with their relative intensity observed in the experiment (6.38: 8.31: 16.36 Fig S1 in Ref. [104]), however, the relative ratio of carbon to oxygen is higher than that obtained in the experiment (the average value from all GQDs of different sizes). In the third step, the mixed system was created and optimized to simulate the mutual electrostatic interaction between GQDs and the surface of Ppy. The supercell contains one GQD that is separated by its periodic image by at least 15 Å.

For this theoretical model of the Ppy/GQD interphase, DFT calculations were performed to obtain information about the energy level distribution and interaction between both composite components. The strength of the interaction between GQDs and Ppy directly depends on their mutual orientation. Another important parameter is the charge transfer between both systems that determines the electronic properties of Ppy/GQDs. The optimized structure of GQDs on the surface of Ppy is presented in Fig. 9 . Binding occurs by intermolecular forces, such as hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces, with a small contribution of π···π stacking interactions. The shortest distances between the oxygen atoms from the adsorbed GQD and the hydrogen atoms from the Ppy polymer are in the range of 1.8–2.7 Å.

The influence of GQDs on the electronic structure of Ppy is also presented. In Fig. 10 , the total and projected density of states (DOS) of the mixed system is shown with the Fermi level set to 0 eV. The plot is limited to the energy range, including the top of the valence band and the bottom of the conduction band. The system exhibits metallic character, where the unoccupied band just above the Fermi level corresponds to both the Ppy and GQD states. The bands extending from approximately 0.5 to 1.2 eV are characteristic of the GQD orbitals only and above 1.2 eV for both systems. This picture clearly indicates that the GQDs participate in the Ppy-involved charge transfer processes. In consequence, oxidation of the mixed Ppy/GQDs system is much easier compared to the polymer itself. Electronic interactions between the polymer and the carbon material are responsible for the significant shift of the redox potential of the PpyNPs interacting directly with the GQDs towards less negative potentials (peak O2).

The redox-active polypyrrole spherical microparticles decorated with graphene quantum dots were produced by chemical oxidative pyrrole polymerization in solution containing surfactant and GQDs. During the synthesis, GQDs are incorporated into the structure of the walls of polypyrrole particles. The presence of GQDs within the polymeric phase significantly affects the structure and electronic properties of composite solid phases deposited at the electrode surface. On the one hand, the composite material has a greater tendency to agglomerate to form a solid phase. This effect that leads to a reduction in the active surface area of ​​the material negatively influences the electrochemical performance of the composite material. On the other hand, this disadvantageous effect is more than completely compensated by changes in the distribution of energy levels on the phase boundary between the polymer and the GQDs. The electronic interaction between both composite components enhances the electron transfer within the electroactive material. As a consequence, the oxidation processes of the polymer phase become much faster than those of the pure polymeric material. The oxidation process of the polymer phase in direct contact with GQDs takes place at potentials less positive than the oxidation potentials of deeper located polymer layers. GQDs also influence the double-layer capacitance current at potentials less positive than potentials of Ppy oxidation. The observed changes in the electrochemical performance of composites related to the influence of GQDs on the charge transfer processes and the capacity of the studied systems are particularly advantageous from the point of view of the use of these composites in electrical energy storage devices. In summary, the PpyNP/GQD composites in this study can potentially provide systems with high specific capacity and fast charging/discharging response. A significant increase in the rate of charge transfer in systems containing GQDs may also significantly affect the redox processes on the surface of the composite. This behavior is particularly advantageous when using studied systems in the electrochemical detection of biologically active compounds.

Bobrowska: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Monika Wysocka-Żołopa: Writing – review & editing. Jakub Goclon: Writing – review & editing. Anna Basa: .
